entry.title: mysql base
entry.content:
create database

CREATE DATABASE dbname;

use this database

USE DATABASE;

drop database or table

DROP DATABASE databasename;
DROP TABLE table_name;

Modifying tables

/* Add a column */
ALTER TABLE tablename
ADD columnname1 datatype constraint;
/* Change a column */
ALTER TABLE tablename
ALTER columnname1 newdatatype newconstraint;
/* Delete a column */
ALTER TABLE tablename
DROP columnname1;

###Adding data

/* Okay to leave out columns */
INSERT INTO tablename
VALUES (value1, value2, value3);

updating data

UPDATE table_name
SET columnname = value;

filtering data with where

Use the UPDATE + WHERE to only update some rows
UPDATE table_name
SET columnname = value
WHERE columnname > 5;
Use the DELETE + WHERE to only delete some rows
DELETE FROM table_name
WHERE columnname IS NULL;

DELETE FROM table_name
WHERE column1 > 1200 AND (column2 = 'hello' OR column3 IS NOT NULL);

finding data

SELCT * FROM table_name WHERE column1 IS NULL;

find data across table

SELECT * FROM tableA INNER JOIN tableB
ON tableA.column1 = tableA.column2
WHERE column3 = 'Hello';

entry.title: useful
entry.content:
∑i=1n(xi−μ)2=∑i=1n(xi−x‾)2+n(x‾−μ)2\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i -\mu)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i -\overline{x})^2 + n(\overline{x} -\mu)^2 i=1∑n​(xi​−μ)2=i=1∑n​(xi​−x)2+n(x−μ)2

where x‾=1n∑i=1nxi\overline{x} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_ix=n1​∑i=1n​xi​ 中间加上 −2nx‾2+nx‾2+nx‾2-2n\overline{x}^2+n\overline{x}^2+n\overline{x}^2−2nx2+nx2+nx2就可以构成


  1. p for "probability", the cumulative distribution function (c. d. f.)
  2. q for "quantile", the inverse c. d. f.
  3. d for "density", the density function (p. f. or p. d. f.)
  4. r for "random", a random variable having the specified distribution

entry.title: bayes prac
entry.content:
Practical 1

study of visitors to Belair National park in July 2019 found that 33018 South Australian visitors booked
a ticket. In the same month, 1205 mobile signals from South Australians were detected in the park.
Assume that the bookings, n = 33018, were the total number of visitors. Treat y = 1205 as the number of
successes. Your model is the Beta prior with binomial likelihood:

P(θ)∝θℵ−1(1−θ)β−1P(\theta) \propto \theta^{\aleph-1}(1-\theta)^{\beta-1} P(θ)∝θℵ−1(1−θ)β−1

and

P(y∣θ)∝θy(1−θ)n−yP(y \mid \theta) \propto \theta^{y}(1-\theta)^{n-y} P(y∣θ)∝θy(1−θ)n−y

Compute un-normalised posteriors and then normalise them
Note: you know or at least can calculate the posterior distribution for this problem. Please don’t do so yet.

  1. Write code that saves the values of alpha, beta, n and y as constants. Discretise theta: represent
    it by at least 100 points between 0 and 1 (e.g. by using seq()). Calculate the product of the prior
    and likelihood, using my functions above for each. This is an (un-normalised) posterior. What is the
    maximum value that it takes?
alpha <- 1
beta <- 1
n <- 33018
y <- 1205
theta <- seq(0,1,by=0.01)
prior <- thetaˆ(alpha-1) * (1-theta)ˆ(beta-1) # or dbeta(theta, alpha, beta)
likelihood <- thetaˆy * (1-theta)ˆ(n-y)
#note dbinom(y,n,theta) does not work with vector theta input
posterior <- prior*likelihood
df <- tibble(theta,prior,likelihood,posterior)
ggplot(df,aes(theta,posterior))+ geom_line()

Work with the conjugate prior

For the example of this prac, the posterior is
θ|y ∼ Beta(α + y, β + n − y) .

\theta \mid \belong

  1. Using an appropriate function (dbeta in R), compute the posterior pdf on your theta.
    posterior_conj <- dbeta(theta,alpha+y, beta+n-y)
    df <- df %>% add_column(posterior_conj)
    df %>% pivot_longer(-theta) %>% ggplot(aes(theta,value,color=name)) + geom_line()
    6

Practical 2

拒绝采样

对于一个难以采样的后验分布，我们可以通过给定一个目标 g(θ)g(\theta)g(θ),使得目标分布能够包含分布f(θ)f(\theta)f(θ) 并找到 最好的k使得 K∗posterior/proposal<1K * posterior / proposal < 1K∗posterior/proposal<1， 一般来讲 proposal g(θ)g(\theta)g(θ)都会采取均匀分布

随机生成x坐标，然后求出概率 (K∗f(θ)/g(θ)K*f(\theta) / g(\theta)K∗f(θ)/g(θ))-> 后验的分布函数值，最后基于x坐标上随机生成y坐标，若当前y坐标小于后验的分布函数值，则接受。
Generates 100000 random number in the range of [−1,1.5][-1,1.5][−1,1.5]
runif(100000,-1,1.5)
95% CI
quantile(accepted_samples,c(0.025,0.975)) #95% CI for mu

Reject sampling code and link

introduction

pacman::p_load(
  tidyverse,
  tidymodels,
  vip
)
football <-read_delim(here::here("data","football.txt"))
football

new_football <-
  football%>%
  transmute(diff =favorite-underdog-spread)

new_football
#oo <-
  #new_football%>%
summ<-summarise(new_football,mean = mean(diff),s.d = sd(diff))
new_football%>%
  ggplot(aes(diff))+
  geom_histogram()

N <- 100000 #number of samples from proposal
mu_proposals <- runif(N,-1,1.5)#started with wide bounds for the proposals, then narrowed them
n<-nrow(new_football)
x_bar<-summ$mean
sigma<-13.7
#At this stage we need K such that Kf (x)/g(x) ≤ 1 for all x. Here f (x) is the posterior, and g(x) is the
#proposal.
#Let me sub in the functions and write that out as a very similar problem.
#̃K exp
#[
#  − (mu_proposals −  ̄x)2
#  2σ2/n
#]
#≤ 1.
#I need to find  ̃K such that the above line is true. You might be confused - what is  ̃K? Answer: I absorbed
#all of the normalising constants for the functions f (x) and g(x) into K. This is the step that I imagine takes
#everyone off guard.
#Try saying the following: if there is a  ̃K satisfying the second equation, then there is a K satisfying the first
#equation. The only difference between the two is the normalising constants for f (x) and g(x). But, to use
#the algorithm I only need to know Kf (x)/g(x), and that is the same thing as
#̃K exp
#[
#  − (mu_proposals −  ̄x)2
 # 2σ2/n
#]
#.
#Now we identify  ̃K: since the left-hand-side of the inequality is e−a2
#, it already is ≤ 1 and I can choose
#̃K = 1. Compute Kf (x)/g(x) for each sample.

# compute K*f/g
Kfgs <- exp(-(mu_proposals-x_bar)^2/(2*sigma^2/n))
probs <- runif(N)
accepted_samples <- mu_proposals[probs < Kfgs]
length(accepted_samples)/N # We accept about 30% of samples
#4. Formally we require g(μ) > 0 whenever f (μ) > 0. Is this assumption satisfied? Is there a problem?
#  Solutions:
#  No - the posterior is formally supported on μ ∈ R but the proposal has defined lower and upper bounds.
#However, not a practical problem. If the lower bound for the proposal density is larger than 1, or if the
#upper bound for the proposal density is smaller than about 1.5, then you may cut off significant parts of the
#posterior (try it). As long as your proposal is supported on at least [-1,1.5], you get well over 99% of the
#posterior mass.

accepted_samples %>% as_tibble() %>% ggplot(aes(accepted_samples, y=..density..)) + geom_histogram()
quantile(accepted_samples,c(0.025,0.975)) #95% CI for mu

filter the wanted information and change date to year only
ad_temp <- temperature %>% filter(city_name=="PORT" & date > "2004-12-31" & date < "2015-01-01") ad_temp <- ad_temp %>% mutate( date = year(date)) #save the date as year only
group by date and get the mean within valid date
remove missing value by using na.rm =TRUE
tem1<- tem%>% group_by(date)%>% summarise(Mean = mean(temperature, na.rm=TRUE))
generate 1 iid value with corrsponde mean and sd
rnorm(1 , mu_mn, mu_sd(mu_sigma[i-1,2])

Week3 gibbs sampler

吉布斯采样：对于一个难以采样的联合分布函数P(x,y)P(x,y)P(x,y)，假设它的条件函数 P(x∣y)P(x \mid y)P(x∣y)和
P(y∣x)P(y \mid x)P(y∣x)能够轻易采样,则我们可以通过交替条件来进行采样.这意味着相应的变量会在迭代的过程中交替使用.

procedure

给定一个难以采样的联合后验分布,我们希望通过gibse sampler来为我们进行采样

p(x∣θ1,θ2)∝θ1x(1−θ1)100−xθ2xexp⁡−θ2p(x \mid \theta_1,\theta_2) \propto \theta_1^x(1-\theta_1)^{100-x}\theta_2^x \exp^{-\theta_2} p(x∣θ1​,θ2​)∝θ1x​(1−θ1​)100−xθ2x​exp−θ2​

假设我们已知他们的条件分布函数为

θ1∣x∼Beta(2+x,103−x)\theta_1 \mid x \sim Beta(2+x,103-x) θ1​∣x∼Beta(2+x,103−x)

θ2∣x∼Γ(x+0.5,1)\theta_2 \mid x \sim \Gamma(x+0.5,1) θ2​∣x∼Γ(x+0.5,1)

则我们可以通过对10000个样本进行采样,并给X0X_0X0​设置一个随机初值,以观测值Xi−1X_{i-1}Xi−1​作为条件从而推断出后验分布。

N <- 10000 #how many samples to take
thetas=matrix(1,nrow=N,ncol=2) #initialise matrix to save our output. This is a good step philosophicall
for(i in 2:N){
thetas[i,1]=rbeta(1, 2+data, 103-data )
thetas[i,2]=rgamma(1,data+0.5,1)
}

example

需要注意的是有的条件函数可能会基于当前时间的另外一个参数

rho=0.75 #correlation
#initialise matrix for samples
theta=matrix(0,nrow=200,ncol=2)
#sample from conditional distributions
for(i in 2:200){
#sample time i, component 1 using time i-1, component 2
theta[i,1]=rnorm(1,rho*theta[i-1,2],sqrt(1-rhoˆ2))
#sample time i, component 2 using i,1
theta[i,2]=rnorm(1,rho*theta[i,1],sqrt(1-rhoˆ2))
}

问题1

对于 MCMC, 我们给马尔科夫链所假设的初值不一定是正确的.尽管最后的数据会趋于收敛.但丢掉一些马尔可夫链的初始步骤可以消除所谓的“磨合期”，即链中有不成比例的许多样本
低概率区域

问题2

如果我们的样本长时间保持相关性，那么看起来像许多样本(100多个)，
实际上可能相当于更少的不相关样本

对于相关性的plot

pacman::p_load(bayesforecast) #contains a ggplot style acf
ggacf(mu_sigma) #formatted as a matrix:

Week 4 Metropolis-Hastings algorithm

When implementing M-H, you need to to know

  1. the posterior density/mass function up to some proportionality constant.
  2. to have a proposal distribution in mind to move around samples of the posterior variables

The second point breaks down as follows:

  1. Choose a rule to move your samples around. I will be estimating variables μ\muμ and σ2\sigma^2σ2. let θ(t)∈R2\theta^(t) \in \mathbb{R^2}θ(t)∈R2 be the t-th sample of (μ,σ2)(\mu,\sigma^2)(μ,σ2): will use the proposal θ(t+1)∣θ(t)∼N(θ(t),cI)\theta^{(t+1)} \mid \theta^{(t)} \sim N(\theta^{(t)},cI)θ(t+1)∣θ(t)∼N(θ(t),cI) for some constant c.
  2. write a function to take one sample of θ(t+1)\theta^{(t+1)}θ(t+1) given an input θ(t)\theta^{(t)}θ(t).
prop_sd <- c(0.1,0.04) #These sds are ad hoc and must be tuned. 
# Once you understand the file, change these numbers and look at the acf plots!

#sample once for mu and once for sigma.
sample_proposal <- function(sample){
  rnorm(2,mean=sample,sd=prop_sd)
}
  1. Write a function to evaluate the proposal density function p(θ(t+1)∣θ(t))p(\theta^{(t+1)} \mid \theta^{(t)})p(θ(t+1)∣θ(t)) at a location given by proposal (θ∗)(\theta^∗)(θ∗), given an input sample (θ(t))(\theta^{(t)})(θ(t)).
prop_dens <- function(sample, proposal){
  prod(dnorm(proposal, mean=sample, sd=prop_sd))
  #above dnorm(...) calculates the density of the two gaussian proposal distributions
  #that is, for mu and for sig2
  #prod(dnorm(...)) finds the density of the proposal as a whole
}

总结：对于Metropolis-Hastings算法

  1. 我们先需要计算出我们的后验分布函数(包括忽略不必要的常数)
  2. 构造一个提议分布来移动后验变量的样本
  • 对于提议分布,第一步我们需要先确定它移动的规则,并创建一个函数去取取θ(t+1)\theta^{(t+1)}θ(t+1)的一个样本
  • 给定输入θ(t)\theta^{(t)}θ(t) (也就是创建马尔科夫链,并使用随机生成分布函数(r开头的)去生成马尔科夫链)
  • 创捷一个函数能够去评估提议密度函数,即p(θ(t+1)∣θ(t))p(\theta^{(t+1)} \mid \theta^{(t)})p(θ(t+1)∣θ(t)) 在提案(θ∗)(\theta^∗)(θ∗)给出的位置，给定输入示例(θ(t))(\theta^{(t)})(θ(t))。
  1. 处理数据
  2. 选定合适的先验值,并计算出似然,先验分布和后验分布
  3. 初始化矩阵
  4. 跑下面的板子
  5. 去除burn-in problem(也就是说要选定一个区间[1,???],去除里面的数据,并检查相关性.

Code for Metropolis-Hastings

generic code for Metropolis-Hastings.

for(i in 2:N){
  #sample from proposal
  proposal <- sample_proposal(samples[i-1,])
  
  #compute probability - possible to get Inf or NaN
  r <- (post_exact( proposal ) /
    post_exact( samples[i-1,] )) *
    (prop_dens( proposal, samples[i-1,] ) /
    prop_dens( samples[i-1,], proposal ))
  
  # Possible to get NaNs, e.g. if the proposal is a negative variance. 
  r[is.nan(r)]=0 #reject. 
  
  #accept or reject step
  prob <- runif(1,0,1)
  if( prob<r ){ #then accept
    samples[i,] = proposal
    n_accept <- n_accept+1
  } else {
    samples[i,] = samples[i-1,]
  } #end else
} #end for loop

#report the percent of accepted steps
percent_accept <- 100*n_accept/N
percent_accept

Part One: Beta-Binomial

In this part you connect Metropolis-Hastings output to your standard Bayesian conjugate priors.

Problem

When recording the sex of babies born in Paris between 1745 and 1770, a total of 241,945 girls and 251,527
boys were recorded. Laplace concluded that he was ‘morally certain’ that the probability θ\thetaθ of birth sex being
female was less than 0.5.
Can you do any better?

  1. Formulate a binomial likelihood - what is n and what is x?
    n is the total number of people, 493472, while x is 241945.
  2. Write down a prior. If you are feeling fancy, use the Fisher information in the binomial distribution to obtain the Jeffreys prior.
    Fisher information for the binomial distribution is n/(θ(1−θ))n/(\theta(1 − \theta))n/(θ(1−θ)), so the Jeffreys prior is p(θ)∝θ−1/2(1−θ)−1/2p(\theta) \propto \theta^{-1/2}(1-\theta)^{-1/2}p(θ)∝θ−1/2(1−θ)−1/2
  3. Write a function, log_post, to compute the log of the posterior up to a constant.
    对后验分布进行log化
x <- 241945
n <- x + 251527
log_post <- function(theta){
(x-1/2)*log(theta) + (n-x-1/2)*log(1-theta)
}
  1. Implement Metropolis-Hastings to get samples from the posterior and plot a histogram. Use the following code to compute the probability r of keeping a sample:
#write a function to do MH:
mh_sampler <- function(
    #inputs
  sample_proposal, #fun to sample from proposal
  prop_dens, #fun to evaluate proposal density
  log_post, #fun to evaluate log(posterior)
  initial_sample, #initial sample
  N = 5e4, #number of samples - default to 50,000
  burnin = 1e4 #burnin to remove - default 10,000 for safety
){
  #steps
  #initialise matrix for samples
  samples=matrix(0,nrow=N,ncol=length(initial_sample))
  samples[1,] = initial_sample
  n_accept <- 0 #count how many proposal steps were accepted
  # Code for Metropolis-Hastings
  for(i in 2:N){
    #sample from proposal
    proposal <- sample_proposal(samples[i-1,])
    #compute probability - possible to get Inf or NaN
    r <- exp(log_post( proposal ) -
               log_post( samples[i-1,] )) *
      (prop_dens( proposal, samples[i-1,] ) /
         prop_dens( samples[i-1,], proposal ))
    # Possible to get NaNs, e.g. if the proposal is a negative variance.
    r[is.nan(r)]=0 #reject.
    #accept or reject step
    prob <- runif(1,0,1)
    if( prob<r ){ #then accept
      samples[i,] = proposal
      n_accept <- n_accept+1
    } else {
      samples[i,] = samples[i-1,]
    } #end else
  } #end for loop
  #report the percent of accepted steps
  percent_accept <- 100*n_accept/N
  #output percent of accepted samples
  print(paste("Percent of accepted samples: ", percent_accept))
  #remove the burn-in
  samples_trunc <- samples[-1:-burnin,]
  #output truncated samples
  return(samples_trunc)
}

5.根据题目要求设计提议样本和提议密度

prop_sd <- 0.005
sample_proposal <- function(theta){rnorm(1,mean=theta,sd=prop_sd)}
prop_dens <- function(theta,proposal){
dnorm(proposal, mean=theta, sd=prop_sd)
}
  1. 检查相关性
    ggacf(samples)

bad correlation

good correlation

entry.title: bayes lecture
entry.content:
Topic 1(week1) formulate Bayes

bayes theorem

Interpretation

Given that the random variable X and y, we can written as p(x|y) ∝\propto∝ p(y|x) p(x).
When the given pdf like p(x|y) written by itself, then in context x is an unknown random variable and y is fixed and known.
Interpret the entire equation as follows: x is unknown random variable and t is fixed and known. The whole Bayesian trick is that P(x∣y)P(x \mid y)P(x∣y)can be used in the right-hand-side to help us learn about x .

Example

p(x|y) is the "posterior" given knowledge of the "linklihood p(y|x) and the "prior" p(x)

  1. Suppose you are given the distributions of y|x and of x.
  2. Find the pdfs/pmfs (standard,given or looked up), multiply them
  3. Recognise the dependence on x in p(x|y) - uniquely specfies a distribution
  4. (Sometimes) Infer the distribution x|y and its parameters
    Consiser some trial with two outcomes,suppose we record n trials with y successes. In what follow we will use y as the data to learn θ\thetaθ, the probability of 'success' in the trial will be binomial mode.
    Bin(y|n,θ\thetaθ) = (ny)θy(1−θ)n−y\binom{n}{y}\theta^{y}(1-\theta)^{n-y}(yn​)θy(1−θ)n−y.

Bayes 2

In the above Conext n is fixed- part of the experimental design. That is: there is no context in which we think of n as unknown or random.
Bayesian habit: for clarity , don't explicitly condition on variables that are always constant. Then rewrite to

Bin(y|θ\thetaθ) = (ny)θy(1−θ)n−y\binom{n}{y}\theta^{y}(1-\theta)^{n-y}(yn​)θy(1−θ)n−y.

important convert

p(θ\thetaθ|y) = p(y∣θ)p(θ)p(y)\frac{p(y|\theta)p(\theta)}{p(y)}p(y)p(y∣θ)p(θ)​

∝\propto∝ θy(1−θ)n−y{\theta^y}(1-\theta)^{n-y}θy(1−θ)n−y

Any factor of the right hand side that does not depent on your predicter can be swallowed into the proportionality constant.

Prior distributions

Say i know nothing about the probability of success in the trial θ\thetaθ, before i measure, the natrual distribution will express as θ\thetaθ ~U(0,1).
The distributions that represent your knowledge before using data are called prior distributions

prior to posterior

Call p(\x|y) (here p(θ\thetaθ|y)) the posterior distribution, in our example

p(θ∣y)∝θy(1−θ)n−yp(\theta|y) \propto {\theta^y}(1-\theta)^{n-y} p(θ∣y)∝θy(1−θ)n−y

is a Beta distribution in θ:θ∣y Beta(y+1,n−y+1)\theta: \theta |y ~ Beta(y+1,n-y+1)θ:θ∣y Beta(y+1,n−y+1)

Conjugate priors

Definition

A family of prior distributions,

P=p(θ),P = {p(\theta)}, P=p(θ),

is said to be conjugate(共轭) to the family of likelihoods,

L=p(y∣θ)\mathcal{L} = {p(y \mid \theta)} L=p(y∣θ)

if
p(θ∣y)∈Pp(\theta \mid y) \in Pp(θ∣y)∈P for all p(θ)∈Pp(\theta) \in Pp(θ)∈P and p(y∈θ)∈Lp(y \in \theta) \in \mathcal{L}p(y∈θ)∈L
which mean if the posterior distribution p(θ∣y)p(\theta \mid y)p(θ∣y) is in the same probability distribution family as the prior probability distribution p(θ)p(\theta)p(θ) ,the prior and posterior are then called conjugate distributions, and the prior is called a conjugate prior for the likelihood function p(y∈θ)p(y \in \theta)p(y∈θ)

proportionality

marginal pdf review

pX(x)=(∫−∞∞p(x,y)  dy),pY(y)=(∫−∞∞p(x,y)  dx)p_{X}(x) =\left(\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} p(x,y) \; dy\right),p_{Y}(y) =\left(\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} p(x,y) \; dx\right) pX​(x)=(∫−∞∞​p(x,y)dy),pY​(y)=(∫−∞∞​p(x,y)dx)

In this course can be written as p(x)=∫p(x,y)  dy,p(y)=∫p(x,y)  dxp(x)= \int p(x,y) \; dy,p(y)= \int p(x,y) \;dxp(x)=∫p(x,y)dy,p(y)=∫p(x,y)dx

Example case: conjugate normal distribution


Restate and we will have:

prior written in bayesian form because we only consider about uuu, therefore any not reliance function can be consider as constant
When finding p(x∣y)p(x \mid y)p(x∣y): y is a constant and x is unknown, all constant terms(not reliance on x) may swallowed into the constant of proportionality
Then:



since we expand (x−u+)2(x-u^{+})^{2}(x−u+)2 to x2−2xu++(u+)2x^{2} - 2xu^{+} +(u^{+})^{2}x2−2xu++(u+)2
we can ignore (u+)2(u^{+})^{2}(u+)2 as it is constant

Week 2 (rejection sampling and priors theory)

Choosing priors

  1. trials with two outcomes -> consider Binomial(or Possion)
  2. bounded below and above -> consider Beta
  3. bounded on one side -> consider Gamma( or Inverse Gamma)
  4. not bounded -> consider Normal

uninformative Priors

A prior that introduces no or little information about the value of the parameter is called an uninformative Priors

Example

if θ\thetaθ is a probability, then in the absence of any prior information, specifying θ U(0,1)\theta ~ U(0,1)θ U(0,1) would seem a reasonable choice.
Suppose x1,x2,...,xn∣μx_{1},x_{2},...,x_{n} \mid \mux1​,x2​,...,xn​∣μ are assumed IID N(μ,σ2\mu, \sigma^{2}μ,σ2) with σ2\sigma^{2}σ2 known and consider the prior μ\muμ ~ N(μ0,τ2)N(\mu_{0}, \tau^{2})N(μ0​,τ2).
One approach to botaining an uninformative prior is to choose an arbitrary value for μ0\mu_{0}μ0​ and let τ2→∞\tau^{2} \to \inftyτ2→∞

Improper priors


so the specification p(μ)∝1p( \mu )\propto 1p(μ)∝1 can be used as if it were a prior density.
For this reason, the prior specification p(μ)∝1p( \mu )\propto 1p(μ)∝1 is called an improper prior. Which means the take the intergral of the prior pdf can not be normalized it. but take the posterior pdf can be normalized it.

uninformative prior for a normal variance

SUppose x1,x2,...,xn∣σ2x_{1},x_{2},...,x_{n} \mid \sigma^{2}x1​,x2​,...,xn​∣σ2 are assumed IID N(μ,σ2)N(\mu, \sigma^{2})N(μ,σ2) with μ\muμ known and consider the prior.

σ2∼InvGamma(v02,v0σ022)\sigma^{2} \sim Inv Gamma(\frac{v_{0}}{2},\frac{v_{0}\sigma_{0}^{2}}{2}) σ2∼InvGamma(2v0​​,2v0​σ02​​)


since

∫0∞1σ2dσ2=+∞\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sigma^2} d\sigma^2 = +\infty ∫0∞​σ21​dσ2=+∞

this is also an improper prior.
Improper priors are okay to use when appropriate, though be careful not to treat/label them a density/distribution

Jeffreys Priors

Transformations and uninformative priors

A conceptual difficulty with uninformative priors arises from transformations.
Suppose 0 < θ\thetaθ < 1 is a parameter for which we have no information.
We have previously stated it would be reasonable to assume θ∼U(0,1)\theta \sim U(0,1)θ∼U(0,1).
Now let ϕ=θ2\phi = \theta^2ϕ=θ2 so that 0 < ϕ\phiϕ < 1 and ,since we have no information about θ\thetaθ, we must also have no information about ϕ\phiϕ.
The previous "principle" would require ϕ∼U(0,1)\phi \sim U(0,1)ϕ∼U(0,1).
But this contradicts θ∼U(0,1)\theta \sim U(0,1)θ∼U(0,1).

Jeffreys' Invariance Principle

To resolve this difficulty, Jeffrets proposed the following principle for constructing uninformative priors.
Consider a problem with parameter θ\thetaθ and likelihood p(X∣θ)p(X \mid \theta)p(X∣θ). The (uninformative) prior should be constructed from p(X∣θ)p(X \mid \theta)p(X∣θ) in such a way that conclusions from the posterior are invariant under transformations of the parameter.

transformations of continuouse variables

Let ϕ=h(θ)\phi = h(\theta)ϕ=h(θ) be a monotonic transformation, then

pϕ(ϕ)=pθ(h−1(ϕ))∣h−1(ϕ)′∣p_{\phi}(\phi) = p_\theta(h^-1(\phi))|h^-1(\phi)^{'}| pϕ​(ϕ)=pθ​(h−1(ϕ))∣h−1(ϕ)′∣

The Jeffreys' Prior

Consider the likelihood p(x∣θ)p(x \mid \theta)p(x∣θ). The prior distribution of the form

p(θ)∝ℓ(θ)p(\theta) \propto \sqrt{\ell(\theta)} p(θ)∝ℓ(θ)​

is called the Jeffreys' Prior, where ℓ(θ)\ell(\theta)ℓ(θ) is the Fisher Information(measured across the data!)


最重要的是数据的期盼值是多少

Example

Suppose x∣θ∼B(n,θ)x \mid \theta \sim B(n,\theta)x∣θ∼B(n,θ).
Recalled that the Fisher information is

ℓ(θ)=nθ(1−θ)\ell (\theta) = \frac{n}{\theta(1-\theta)} ℓ(θ)=θ(1−θ)n​

Here the Jeffreys' Prior is defined by

p(θ)∝θ−12(1−θ)−12p(\theta) \propto \theta^{-\frac{1}{2}} (1-\theta)^{-\frac{1}{2}} p(θ)∝θ−21​(1−θ)−21​

or, equivalently ,

θ∼Beta(12,12)\theta \sim Beta(\frac{1}{2},\frac{1}{2}) θ∼Beta(21​,21​)

Monte Carlo Sampling

simulate a posterior distribution

example: the posterior distribution is θ∼Beta(438,544)\theta \sim Beta(438,544)θ∼Beta(438,544)
Step

  1. plot the exact posterior distribution (for comparsion)
  2. sample many times from the posterior
  3. compare: histogram vs pdf, means. 95% CIs
    Simulate samples from Beta(438,544), draw a histogram with quantiles, and do the same for a transformed variable.

    round it to 0.375 to 0.525 and get

    Then we take the sample from Beta(438,544),n=100

    when n and bin increasing, the sample distribution will prefect match to the actual distribution.

Rejection sampling

Rejection sampling is a method for generating random observations from a density, f(θ)f(\theta)f(θ), that we cannot sample directly.
The main assumption is the existence of a known density, g(θ)g(\theta)g(θ) such that:

  1. it is easy to sample observations from the density g(θ)g(\theta)g(θ)
  2. you can explicity calculate a constant, K, such that Kf(θ)≤g(θ)Kf(\theta) \le g(\theta)Kf(θ)≤g(θ) for all θ\thetaθ

Formal algorithm

To sample form f(θ)f(\theta)f(θ)

  1. Draw a single observation , θ\thetaθ, from g.
  2. Accept (keep) a sample θ^\hat \thetaθ^ with probability Kf(θ)g(θ)\frac{Kf(\theta)}{g(\theta)}g(θ)Kf(θ)​ abd reject (do not keep) with probability 1−Kf(θ)g(θ)1-\frac{Kf(\theta)}{g(\theta)}1−g(θ)Kf(θ)​
  3. Repeat steps 1 and 2 until an observation is accepted.

给定一个不太好取样的分布，我们希望通过Kf(θ)≤g(θ)Kf(\theta) \le g(\theta)Kf(θ)≤g(θ)得到预估合理的点，并通过公式Kf(θ)/g(θ)Kf(\theta) /g(\theta)Kf(θ)/g(θ)得到当前分布的预估值，这样子就可以对比当前的点是否在分布范围之内。

exaple

Intro

Come up with a proposal g

Easiest idea - g is a uniform density, and choose K=minθg(θ)/p(θ∣x)K = min_{\theta}g(\theta)/p(\theta \mid x)K=minθ​g(θ)/p(θ∣x).

k is the highest around value in the y-axis in graph


run 1 number of sample of x-axis using runif(1)
then compute f and g;
comeup with fg,
then run 1 number of probability on the y-axis. then come up with the conclusion that whether we should accept the sample or not.

拒绝采样

对于一个难以采样的后验分布，我们可以通过给定一个目标 g(θ)g(\theta)g(θ),使得目标分布能够包含分布f(θ)f(\theta)f(θ) 并找到 最好的k使得 K∗posterior/proposal<1K * posterior / proposal < 1K∗posterior/proposal<1， 一般来讲 proposal g(θ)g(\theta)g(θ)都会采取均匀分布

随机生成x坐标，然后求出概率 (K∗f(θ)/g(θ)K*f(\theta) / g(\theta)K∗f(θ)/g(θ))-> 后验的分布函数值，最后基于x坐标上随机生成y坐标，若当前y坐标小于后验的分布函数值，则接受。

Topic 3(week3) from MC to MCMC and gibbs sampler

The problem with rejection sampling

  1. Rejection sampling in difficult problems gets harder: you have to devise a good proposal density.
  2. Samples aren't correlated: if i find a region of high probability in one sample, that information is not used in the future.
    What if we did use it? When we find a good sample, we could hang around nearby and explore the distribution.

key elements in markov chain monte carlo

Use one sample to determine how to draw the next sample: thhs is a Markov Chain.
We'll still have a 'proposal density': it will be the way we determine the next sample from the current sample.
Design the Markov Chain so that it converges to the posterior distribution.

Accuracy of Simple Monte Carlo

Sample size for simple Monte Carlo

Large sample sizes may be required for some quantites, such as probabilities of rare events or extreme quantiles of the posterior distribution.
For example, if the posterior probability p of a certain event θ∈A\theta \in Aθ∈A is small, then to obtain a simple Monte Carlo esimate of p with a standard error of p/2 would required

S≈4/pS \approx 4/p S≈4/p

Markov chain

A discrete time Markov chain with countable state space is a sequence of random variables

X1,X2,X3,..X_1,X_2,X_3, .. X1​,X2​,X3​,..

that satisfies the property

P(Xt=xt∣Xt−1=xt−,Xt−2=xt−1,...,X1=x1)=P(Xt=xt∣Xt−1=xt−1)P(X_t = x_t \mid X_{t-1} = x_{t-},X_{t -2} = x_{t-1} ,...,X_1 =x_1) = P(X_t = x_t \mid X_{t-1} = x_{t-1}) P(Xt​=xt​∣Xt−1​=xt−​,Xt−2​=xt−1​,...,X1​=x1​)=P(Xt​=xt​∣Xt−1​=xt−1​)

Time Homogeneous

A Markov chain is said to be time homogeneous if

(Xt=xj∣Xt−1=xi)(X_t = x_j \mid X_{t-1} = x_{i}) (Xt​=xj​∣Xt−1​=xi​)

does not depend on t

example

Transition matrices

For a Markov chain with finite state space, the process can be represented by the transition matrix
P=[pij]\mathbb{P} =[p_{ij}]P=[pij​] where pij=P(Xt=xj∣Xt−1=xi)p_ij = P(X_t = x_j \mid X_{t-1} = x_i)pi​j=P(Xt​=xj​∣Xt−1​=xi​)
In what follows ,we will consider only Markov chain with finite state space X\mathcal{X}X
let π\piπ be a row vector of probabilities over X\mathcal{X}X so that

πi≥0and∑iπi=1\pi_i \ge 0 and \sum_{i} \pi_i =1 πi​≥0andi∑​πi​=1

if the initial state X0X_0X0​ is chosen according to π\piπ, then the distribution of XtX_tXt​ is

πPt\pi \mathbb{P}^t πPt


Irreducible Markov chain

Periodicity

The period of a state, is defined to be

gcd(n>0:pn(x,x)>0)gcd ({{ n>0 : p_n(x,x) >0}}) gcd(n>0:pn​(x,x)>0)

It can be shown that all states in a communicating class must have the same period.
The period of an irreducible chain is therefore the period of any state x \in \matchcal{X}

Aperiodic Chains

These are what we care about! An irreducible Markov chain with period 1 is said to be aperiodic. Note that if p1(x,x)>0p_1(x,x) > 0p1​(x,x)>0, tehn the Markov Chain is aperiodic( since gcd(1,n) =1)

Stationarity


The Gibbs Sampler

The Gibbs Sampler is one of the simplest methods of constructing a Markov chain with a given stationary distribution.
Consider a p-dimensional variable, θ=(θ1,θ2,...,θp)\theta = (\theta_1,\theta_2, ... ,\theta_p)θ=(θ1​,θ2​,...,θp​) and a target probability density p(θ)p(\theta)p(θ).
The transition from θt\theta^tθt to θt+1\theta^{t+1}θt+1 is performed in p steps by sampling θj(t+1)\theta_j^{(t+1)}θj(t+1)​ form

p(θj∣θ1(t+1),...,θj−1(t+1),θj+1(t),...,θp(t))p(\theta_j \mid \theta_1^{(t+1)},..., \theta_{j-1}^{(t+1)}, \theta_{j+1}^{(t)},..., \theta_p^{(t)}) p(θj​∣θ1(t+1)​,...,θj−1(t+1)​,θj+1(t)​,...,θp(t)​)

for j=1,2,...,pj = 1,2,...,pj=1,2,...,p

example

Key Issues for MCMC 1

Burn-in: since your initial condition for the Markov Chain is not "correct", i.e. not drawn from the stationary distribution ( which we don't know or we wouldn't be using the Gibbs sampler), your initial samples might no fit the distribution.
Burn-in: in theory no problem since the Markov Chain converges anyway. Best practice is trying to start your MCMC in a region of high probability. As secondary, discarding some initial steps from your Markov Chain can remove the so-called "burn-in" period where your Chain has disproportionately many samples in low probability regions.

Key Issues for MCMC 2

Correlation: Our samples are correlated for several steps. The issue is that if our samples stay correlated for a long time, then waht looks like many samples(100+), might actually be equivalent to many fewer uncorrelated samples.
Our samples are correlated for several steps. We'll need a diagnostic for the Effective Sample Size(ESS). The ESS estimates how many independent samples our MCMC sample is equivalent to: for S MCMC sample, 1≤ESS≤S1 \le ESS \le S1≤ESS≤S

Key Issues for MCMC 3

If the posterior distribution is multimodal with "islands" of high density surrounded by regions of very low density, transitions between the high density regions may require an unrealistically high number of steps.

Week 4 Metropolis Hastings

Motivation

In rejection sampling we took a hard sampling problem(posterior) and replaced it with an easier sampling problem(proposal) and a 'tweaking' step(accept/reject)
Noew we're trying to use Markov chains to get our samples, we've hit a hard problem: how to write down the Markov chain?

Idea

Reminder: we want to get samples θ(t)\theta^{(t)}θ(t)
Define my 'simple' Markov Chain by the density function of θ(t)\theta^{(t)}θ(t) given θ(t−1)\theta^{(t-1)}θ(t−1): at step t, choose a new state θ∗\theta^{*}θ∗ according to a simple proposal distribution J(θ∣θ(t−1))J(\theta \mid \theta^{(t-1)})J(θ∣θ(t−1))
Example: choose θ∗\theta^{*}θ∗ by sampling from a normal distribution with mean θ(t−1)\theta^{(t-1)}θ(t−1)

Correction step

Properties of Metropolis-Hastings

Why does it work?

  1. note irreducibility of the Markov Chain is fine as long as
    repeated iterations of the proposal Markov Chain could reach
    anywhere
  2. aperiodicity of the Markov chain is free for r<1r < 1r<1, or for r=1r = 1r=1
    if the proposal has J(θ,θ)>0.J(\theta, \theta) > 0.J(θ,θ)>0.
  3. now check the stationary distribution is correct

Gibbs sampler

gibbs sampler i s a special case of Metropolis-Hastings when its proposal function equal to 1

Week 5 (Stan)

The Bayesian workflow

  1. Choose prior/likelihood.
  2. Write code to sample from posterior
  3. Tune/diagnose/fix code in 2.
  4. Do posterior predictive checking to confirm all prior steps

Stan workflow

  1. Write,get or import data in R
  2. write a .stan file that lists the variables you are trying to estimate, how those variables relate to your data, and your prior information

Example

Define data

Define parameters

Define transformed parameters

e.g. want standard deviation and the variance

Define model

Week 6

Exchangeability

if a iid variable ZiZ_iZi​ is a collection of experiment data, we say ZiZ_iZi​ is exchangeable if joint distribution p(z1,z2,z3,...,zn)p(z_1,z_2,z_3,...,z_n)p(z1​,z2​,z3​,...,zn​) does not change when we can arbitrary re-order the elements of Z=(z1,z2,z3,...,zn)Z=(z_1,z_2,z_3,...,z_n)Z=(z1​,z2​,z3​,...,zn​)
ZZZ can be split into two variable(x,y)i(x,y)_i(x,y)i​ where xix_ixi​ refers to explanatory variable and yiy_iyi​ refers to the rest of ZiZ_iZi​
把数据 Z分成（x，y），若Z是iid和 exchangeable，那么（x，y）也是

Explanatory

definition: the components of ZiZ_iZi​ that we do not model as random.
Let X−>xiX ->x_iX−>xi​ be the set of explanatory variables in ZiZ_iZi​
Them for yi∣xi,p(xi,yi)y_i \mid x_i , p(x_i,y_i)yi​∣xi​,p(xi​,yi​) is exchangeable

zi=(hi,vi,ei)z_i=(h_i,v_i,e_i)zi​=(hi​,vi​,ei​) represent x-axis,y-axis,and density.

If we given variable yiy_iyi​, where yiy_iyi​ is some subset of data, then it is usually appropriate to identify subset of the explanatory variable X−>xiX -> x_iX−>xi​ and call that subset the predictors θ\thetaθ.
We implicitly condition yiy_iyi​ on xix_ixi​

把实验数据分成 y(outcome), 并建模把他看成是random variable, x(explanatory) 并把他们分成 predictor(θ\thetaθ) and 协变量covariate(C).

有时候我们的后验分布可能会基于data中的一些其他信息(例如n代表着数量,属于协变量covariate),在 baysian里面,我们通常为了更加清晰的计算,会忽略掉covariate.

Post.Pred.distribution


若预测 y^\hat{y}y^​是基于y的,那么y的联合分布p(y,θ)p(y,\theta)p(y,θ)也是基于x的.(marginal distribution of y)
因为y^\hat{y}y^​和y是具有可交换性的,所以y直接忽略

using P(a∣b)P(a \mid b)P(a∣b)

Rejection sampler

multi_nuisance


得到边界函数 P(θ1∣y)P(\theta_1 \mid y)P(θ1​∣y) 等同于对 P(θ1,θ2∣y)P(\theta_1,\theta_2 \mid y)P(θ1​,θ2​∣y)求基于θ2\theta_2θ2​积分 ,通过基于那个数值的积分,我们可以得到p(θ2∣y)p(\theta_2 \mid y)p(θ2​∣y),并以这个函数为基础去求他们的其他conditional probability. 也就是P(θ1,θ2∣y)=P(θ1,θ2,y)/p(y)P(\theta_1,\theta_2 \mid y) = P(\theta_1,\theta_2 , y) / p(y)P(θ1​,θ2​∣y)=P(θ1​,θ2​,y)/p(y)
或者说,因为我们想知道p(θ1)p(\theta_1)p(θ1​),所以把p(θ1)p(\theta_1)p(θ1​)永远放在第一位

multi_hierarchical model


because they are assum in independent

using hierarchical model when it have the repeat trials

Week 7

hierarchical Bayes


because θ1\theta_1θ1​ is base on ϕ\phiϕ, when we already knowning θ1\theta_1θ1​(which base on ϕ\phiϕ), we can write ϕ\phiϕ as covariance as θ1\theta_1θ1​ is base on ϕ\phiϕ. But due to θ1\theta_1θ1​ is getting from ϕ\phiϕ, if we known θ1\theta_1θ1​ then ϕ\phiϕ is useless. we can also write as Yi∣θ1Y_i \mid \theta_1Yi​∣θ1​

p(y⃗∣θ1)p(θ1∣ϕ)p(θ2∣ϕ)…p(θn∣ϕ)p(ϕ)p(\vec{y} \mid \theta_1)p(\theta_1 \mid \phi) p(\theta_2 \mid \phi) \dots p(\theta_n \mid \phi) p(\phi) p(y​∣θ1​)p(θ1​∣ϕ)p(θ2​∣ϕ)…p(θn​∣ϕ)p(ϕ)

=p(y1∣θ1)p(y2∣θ2)…p(yn∣θn)×p(θ1∣ϕ)p(θ2∣ϕ)…p(θn∣ϕ)p(ϕ)= p(y_1 \mid \theta_1) p(y_2 \mid \theta_2) \dots p(y_n \mid \theta_n)\times p(\theta_1 \mid \phi) p(\theta_2 \mid \phi) \dots p(\theta_n \mid \phi) p(\phi) =p(y1​∣θ1​)p(y2​∣θ2​)…p(yn​∣θn​)×p(θ1​∣ϕ)p(θ2​∣ϕ)…p(θn​∣ϕ)p(ϕ)

Posterior Predictive Checking (Stan)

entry.title: Data science 3 lecture
entry.content:
Week 1

Prediction

We can predict Y using

Y^=f^(x)\hat{Y} = \hat{f}(x) Y^=f^​(x)

wheref^\hat{f}f^​ is our estimate for f, and $\hat{Y} is our prediction for Y. In this case f^\hat{f}f^​ is a black box.

Parametric method

need to use tranining data to fit / train the model

non-parametric method

data is the model, do not make explicit assumptions about functional form of f. will seek estimate of f as close to dat as possible without too wiggly.

Supervised versus unsupervised learninng

supervised is what you expect to see and for each observed predictor we will have an associated response variable
unsupervised will not have an associated response variable, instead we trying to understand the relationships between the variables and/or observations.

Regression versus classification problems

Regression

Quantitative response -> will be a number

Classification

Qualitative response -> will be a label

Measuring the quality of fit

In the regression stting, mean square error(MSE) will be used;

MSE=1n∑i=1n(yi−f^(xi))2MSE = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_{i} - \hat{f}(x_{i}))^2 MSE=n1​i=1∑n​(yi​−f^​(xi​))2

used it on training data will call training MSE

Test MSE

MSEtest=AVE((f^(x0)−y0)2)MSE_{test} = AVE((\hat{f}(x_{0}) - y_{0})^2) MSEtest​=AVE((f^​(x0​)−y0​)2)

Bias-variance trade-off

E[(Y0−(f^(x0))2]=Var(f^(x0))+(Bias(f^(x0)))2+Var(ε)E[(Y_{0}-(\hat{f}(x_{0}))^2] = Var(\hat{f}(x_{0}))+(Bias(\hat{f}(x_{0})))^2+Var(\varepsilon) E[(Y0​−(f^​(x0​))2]=Var(f^​(x0​))+(Bias(f^​(x0​)))2+Var(ε)

Variance and Bias of f^\hat{f}f^​

variance of f^\hat{f}f^​ will change if we estimate it with a different dataset,more flexible models have have higher variance.
The bias of f^\hat{f}f^​ is

E[f^−f]E[\hat{f} -f ] E[f^​−f]

Epliogue, As flexibility increases, then variance increases, and bias decrease.

Week 2(regression recap)

linear regression recap(regression)

MLR


x11x_{11}x11​ to x1px_{1p}x1p​ is the predictor, y is the response variable

MLR matrix form


where β0\beta _{0}β0​ is the intercept term (if one is included in the model)

Least Squares Estimates

The least square estimate, β^\hat {\beta}β^​ is the vector that minimises the sum of squares

Q(β)=∣∣y−Xβ∣∣2Q(\beta) =||y - X\beta||^{2} Q(β)=∣∣y−Xβ∣∣2

where is the squared distance between the vector y and the linear combination β\betaβ of the colums of the matrix X
The variance σ2\sigma^{2}σ2 is estimated by

se2=1n−p−1∣∣y−Xβ∣∣2s_{e}^{2} = \frac{1}{n-p-1}||y - X\beta||^{2} se2​=n−p−11​∣∣y−Xβ∣∣2

where p+1 is the number of columns of X

Theorem

If the columns of X are linearly independent then the least squares estimates are given uniquely by

β^=(XTX)−1XTy\hat{\beta} =(X^{T}X)^{-1}X^{T}y β^​=(XTX)−1XTy

important questions

Q1


Where TSS donates as total sum square and RSS donates as residual sum square

Q2: Deciding on important variables

Quality of model: AIC,BIC,AICc, adjusted R2R^{2}R2.
Approaches:

  1. Forward selection: Start with null and add.
  2. Backward selection: Start with null and remove.
  3. Mixed selection: start with null and add, but can also remove.
    The mixed selection is the best on reduce the deviation but will increase the time complexity

Q3: Model fit

Classical measures are

R2=TSS−RSSTSSR^{2} = \frac{TSS-RSS}{TSS} R2=TSSTSS−RSS​

and

RSE=RSSn−p−1RSE = \sqrt{\frac{RSS}{n-p-1}} RSE=n−p−1RSS​​

we want RSE smaller as possible adn R2R^{2}R2 bigger as possible, but problem is that the variable may not relevate and it may model noise.

Q4: Predictions


The different is the prediction interval will be individuals and add 1 more than the average

Potential problems

Non-linearity of data

  1. Residual versus fitted plot.
  2. MLR : residual versus each predictor.

Correlation of error terms

we assume that the error terms are uncorrelated. If correlated then estmated standard errors of estimators ten to underestimate the true standard errors. This leads to confidence interval and prediction intervals that are narrower than should be.

Common cause of correlation
  1. Clustering, data which have the identical things.
  2. temporal , data that have observations close to in time
  3. spatial, data that have close space.

Non-constant variance of error terms

σ2\sigma^{2}σ2 does not indenpent on individuals.

Transformation

Try transforming response variables, i.e.log(Y),Yi.e. log(Y), \sqrt{Y}i.e.log(Y),Y​

Weighted least squares


Influential points

Residual represent the dash line we have this point in.
High leverage means when it comes to the predictors, it will long way away.

  1. Large Residual , High Leverage the point is outside of the control of one, and it cause a problem
  2. Small Residual, High Leverage
  3. Large Residual , low Leverage
  4. Small Residual, low Leverage

Collineary

Collinearity refers to the case where two predictors are closely related.
Collinearity can cause the standard errors of the coefficient estimates to grow.

Variance inflation factor(VIF)

The VIF is the ratio of the variance of βj^\hat{\beta_{j}}βj​^​ when fitting the full model divided by the variance of βj^\hat{\beta_{j}}βj​^​ if fit on its own.
Smallest possible vaue is 1.
A VIF greater than 5 indicates problems.

Bias-variance trade-off

  1. validation tends to overestimate test MSE.
  2. LOOCV tends to give unbiased estimates of test MSE.
  3. k-fold CV between the two.
  4. LOOCV has higher variance than k-fold CV as MSE are highly(positively) correlated.
    Rule of thumb is that k-fold is often preferred with k=5 or k=10

Set up

S={X1,X2,…,Xn}S = \{X_1,X_2, \dots, X_n\} S={X1​,X2​,…,Xn​}

and we are interested in a statisctic T=t(S)T = t(S)T=t(S) which is an estimator of the population parameter θ\thetaθ.
We can estimate the sampling distribution of T empirically using bootstrapping

Procedure

Key idea

The population is to the sample, as the sample is to the bootstrap sample.
i.e. the distribution of Tb∗T_b^*Tb∗​ around the original estimate T is analogous to the sampling distribution of the estimator T around the population parameter θ\thetaθ

Week 5

Model selection

Regression setting

The standard linear model:

Y=β0+β1X1+⋯+βpXp+ϵY = \beta_0 +\beta_1X_1+\cdots + \beta_pX_p+\epsilon Y=β0​+β1​X1​+⋯+βp​Xp​+ϵ

usually fit via least squares.

Why alternatives

** Prediction accuracy**

  1. if n>pn>pn>p, then least squares esimates tend to have low variance.
  2. if n is not much larget than p cna get overfitting and poor prediction.
  3. if p>n then no longer a unique fit and infinite variance.
    We can compensate by constraining or shrinking the estimated coefficients so that we can decrease the variance for a small amount o fbias.
    ** Moel interpretability*
    Sometimes we end up with irrelevant variables in the model, by removing these we have a more easily interpretable mode.

Possible alternatives

  1. Subset Selection
  2. Shrinkage
  3. Dimension Reduction

Best subset selection

  1. fit a separate linear regression for each possible combination of p predictors.
  2. Choose the best model

Using Leaps package to achieve beset subset selection algorithm


if want to using forwards stepwise or backward, add method ="forwards"

Choosing the optimal model

Goal

The goal is ideally to chose a model with the lowest test error. To achieve this we need to estimate the test error. This is done by two approaches:

  1. Indirectly estimate test error by adjusting the training error.
  2. Directly estimate test error using a validation set, or cross-validation.

CpC_pCp​

Mallow's CpC_pCp​

Akaike information criterion(AIC)

The AIC is defined as

AIC=2k−2ln(L^)AIC = 2k - 2 ln(\hat {L}) AIC=2k−2ln(L^)

where k is the number of parameters in the model, and ln(L^)ln(\hat{L})ln(L^) is the log likelihood evaluated at the maximum likelihood estimates. We choose the model with the lowest AIC.
*** In addition, the number of parameters in the model is the number of coefficients + the residual standard error. in the OQ5 will be 16+1 =17

Akaike information criterion corrected(AICc)

To adjust for small sample sizes, the AICc is used and it defined as

AICc=AIC+2k(k+1)n−k−1AICc = AIC +\frac{2k(k+1)}{n-k-1} AICc=AIC+n−k−12k(k+1)​

where n is the sample size.

Bayesian information criterion(BIC)

A more stringent criterion with respect to the number of parameters is teh Bayesian information criterion(BIC). it is defined as

BIC=ln(n)k−2ln(L^)BIC = ln(n)k - 2ln(\hat{L}) BIC=ln(n)k−2ln(L^)

always pick the lowest one.

Adjusted R2R^2R2

Shrinkage methods

In shrinkage methods, we fit a model with all p predictors, but constrain or regularise the coefficient estimate ( equivalent to shrinking the estimates towards zero)

Least squares

Ridge regression

Shrinkage penalty

The term

λ∑j=1pβj2\lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j^2 λj=1∑p​βj2​

is called the shrinkage penalty and shrinks the estimates of βj\beta_jβj​ towards zero. Note that the shrinkage penalty is not applied to the intercept.

Example

Scale invariance

Ridge versus least squares

  1. As λ\lambdaλ increase, then the flexibility of the model decreases and so bias increases, but variance decreases..
  2. Also for a givven choice of λ\lambdaλ only a single model is fit and so decrease computation compared to subset selection.

Selecting λ\lambdaλ

This can be achieved by using cross-validation to find the value of λ\lambdaλ that minimises the estimated test MSE.

Week 6

Lasso regression

Ridge regression

In ridge regression, the coefficients are shrunk towards zero, but it will not set any to zero.

Lasso regression

In Lasso regression, the coefficient estimates βj^L\hat{\beta_j}^Lβj​^​L are those that minimise

Norms

  1. Ridge - penalty is based on the ℓ2\ell_2ℓ2​ norm:

∥β∥2=∑j=1pβj2\lVert \beta \rVert _2 = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j^2} ∥β∥2​=j=1∑p​βj2​​

  1. Lasso - penalty is ℓ1\ell_1ℓ1​ norm:

∥β∥1=∑j=1p∣βj∣\lVert \beta \rVert _1 = \sum_{j=1}^{p} |\beta_j| ∥β∥1​=j=1∑p​∣βj​∣

Effect on regression

The lasso shrinks the coefficient estimates towards zero, and if λ\lambdaλ is sufficiently large,it will set some to zero.

Example

when mixture = 1, will set it to lasso, if mixture =0,set it to ridge

Alternative formulations


Geometric interpretation

In the case where p=2p=2p=2, then the ridge coefficients are those that give the smallest RSS that are contained in the circle

β12+β22≤s\beta_1^2 + \beta_2^2 \le s β12​+β22​≤s

and the lasso coefficients are those that give the smallest RSS of those in the diamond defined by

∣β1∣+∣β2∣≤s|\beta_1| + |\beta_2| \le s ∣β1​∣+∣β2​∣≤s

Subset selection


A special case

Consider case when n=pn=pn=p and X=In×nX = I_{n \times n}X=In×n​, we also simplify more by fit a no-intercept mode, i.e.i.e.i.e.,

RSS=∑j=1p(yj−βj)2RSS = \sum_{j=1}^{p} (y_j - \beta_j)^2 RSS=j=1∑p​(yj​−βj​)2

Least squares

βj^=yj\hat{\beta_j} = y_j βj​^​=yj​

Ridge

βj^=yj1+λ\hat{\beta_j} =\frac{y_j}{1+\lambda} βj​^​=1+λyj​​

Lasso

second line should be −λ/2-\lambda /2−λ/2

Bayesian interpretation

Consider the posterior distribution of β:\beta:β:

f(β∣X,Y)∝f(Y∣X,β)f(β)f(\beta \mid X,Y) \propto f(Y \mid X,\beta)f(\beta) f(β∣X,Y)∝f(Y∣X,β)f(β)

Also assume that

f(β)=∏j=1pg(βj)f(\beta) = \prod_{j=1}^{p}g(\beta_j) f(β)=j=1∏p​g(βj​)

Normal priors

If we assume that prior for β\betaβs is a normal distribution with mean 0 and standard deviation a function of λ\lambdaλ, then the posterior mode and mean of β\betaβ is given by the ridge regression solutions.

Laplace priors

If we assume that prior for β\betaβs is a double exponential(Laplace) distribution with mean 0 and scale parameter a function of λ\lambdaλ, then the posterior mode and mean of β\betaβ is given by the lasso regression solutions.

Step function

Set up

Polynomial regression imposes a global structure on the data, step functions allow a more local structure.
We will break the data into bins and fit a different constant for each bin.

Notation

Constrains

For any given value of X,

C0(X)+C1(X)+⋯+CK(X)=1C_0(X) +C_1(X)+ \dots + C_K(X) = 1 C0​(X)+C1​(X)+⋯+CK​(X)=1

Now we can fit model where

Yi=β0+β1C1(Xi)+⋯+βKCK(Xi)+εiY_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1C_1(X_i)+ \dots +\beta_KC_K(X_i)+\varepsilon_i Yi​=β0​+β1​C1​(Xi​)+⋯+βK​CK​(Xi​)+εi​

Achieve in R



Basis functions and splines

Basis functions

Polynomial and piecewise-constant functions are special cases of the basis function approach to modelling.
Consider a family of cuntions or transformation.

b1(X),b2(X),…,bK(X)b_1(X),b_2(X), \dots , b_K(X) b1​(X),b2​(X),…,bK​(X)

We can fit the model

Yi=β0+β1C1(Xi)+⋯+βKCK(Xi)+εiY_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1C_1(X_i)+ \dots +\beta_KC_K(X_i)+\varepsilon_i Yi​=β0​+β1​C1​(Xi​)+⋯+βK​CK​(Xi​)+εi​

Special cases

Polynomial

bj(xi)=xijb_j(x_i) = x_i^j bj​(xi​)=xij​

Piecewise-constant

bj(xi)=I(cj≤xi<cj+1)b_j(x_i) = I(c_j \leq x_i < c_{j+1}) bj​(xi​)=I(cj​≤xi​<cj+1​)

and basis funtion are fixed and known.

Regression splines

Piecewise Polynomials

Fit separate low-degree polynomials over different regions of X.
The points where the coefficients change are called knots

In this case, our knots is a single knots and is c, and we have eight parameters to estimate

Constraints and splines

The jumps in the fitted piecewise-cubic can be addressed byu imposing the constraints that the function in continuous.
Also, we can ensure that the fitted curve is smooth by ensuring that the first and second derivatives are continuous.
A piecewise cubic with these constraints is called a cubic spline
A cubic spline with K knots uses 4 + K degress of freedom.

Degree-d spline

A degree-d spline is a piecewise degree-d polynomial with continuity in derivatives up to degree d-1

Spline basis representation





step_bs represent basis spline, default value will be cubic

Natural splines

Splines can have high variance at the boundaries of X, A natural spline is a regression spline with additional boundaty constraints: the function is linear at the boundary.
A natural cubic spline with K knots uses K degrees of freedom.

Smoothing splines

Goal

We would like to find a function g(x) that fits the data well, i.e. we would like to minimise

RSS=∑i=1n(yi−g(xi))2RSS = \sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - g(x_i))^2 RSS=i=1∑n​(yi​−g(xi​))2

But if we are not careful we will end up with a g(x) that interpolates all of y_i

Adding a smoothing constraint

We can try and ensure that g(x) is smooth by choosing *g(x) that minimises

∑i=1n(yi−g(xi))2+λ∫g′′(t)2dt\sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - g(x_i))^2 + \lambda \int g^{''}(t)^2dt i=1∑n​(yi​−g(xi​))2+λ∫g′′(t)2dt

where λ>0\lambda > 0λ>0 is a tuning parameter. The function g that minimises this is called a smoothing spline.

Loss + penalty

Effective degrees of freedom

A smoothing spline has n parameters and hence n degrees of freedom, but because of the effect of the smoothing parameter constraints on the parameters we consider the effective degrees of freedom denoted dfλdf_\lambdadfλ​

Calculating dfλdf_\lambdadfλ​

Consider a solution to the optimization for a smoothing spline for a given value of λ\lambdaλ denoted gλ^\hat{g_\lambda}gλ​^​:

gλ^=Sλy\hat{g_\lambda} = S_\lambda y gλ​^​=Sλ​y

So gλ^\hat{g_\lambda}gλ​^​ is the n fitted values
SλS_\lambdaSλ​ is therefore a n×nn \times nn×n matrix. Then the effective degrees of freedom are defined to be

dfλ=∑i=1n[Sλ]iidf_\lambda = \sum_{i=1}^n[S_\lambda]_{ii} dfλ​=i=1∑n​[Sλ​]ii​

i.e. trace of SλS_\lambdaSλ​

Week 8

Multivariate adaptive regression spline (MARS)

details

hinge function

Model fitting

Process involves two phases:

  1. forward pass, and
  2. backward pass

Forward pass

  1. Start with null model - just intercept.
  2. Adds basis functions in pairs to model - choosing the pair that give the largest reduction in RSS. The pairs are the mirrors of one another. Each new basis is a term already in the model multiplied by a hinge.
  3. Explores existing terms, all variables, and all values of variables.
  4. Coefficients for basis are fitted with linear regression.

Backwards pass

  1. Terms are removed one-by-one based on generalised cross validation (GCV)

GCV

Generalized additive models give a framework for extending linear models by allowing non-linear functions of each of the variables while maintaining additivity.

Backfitting

The smoothing splines version is fitted using backfitting which involves repeated updating the fit for each predictor while holding the other predictors fixed. This is achieved by using a partial residual

  4. Smoothness is summarised by degrees of freedom

A common model to describle the relationship between uptake rate and concentration is the Michaelis-Menten model:

f(x,(K,Vm))=VmxK+xf(x, (K,V_m)) = \frac{V_mx}{K+x} f(x,(K,Vm​))=K+xVm​x​

for the data model, we can assume that

Yi=VmxK+x+ϵiY_i = \frac{V_mx}{K+x} + \epsilon_i Yi​=K+xVm​x​+ϵi​

Building a regression tree

  1. Divide the predictor space - X1,X2,…,XpX_1,X_2 , \dots , X_pX1​,X2​,…,Xp​ into J dostomct amd mpm-overlapping regions: R1,R2,…,RjR_1,R_2, \dots, R_jR1​,R2​,…,Rj​
  2. For every observation that falls into region RjR_jRj​ we predict to have the value that is the mean of the responses of all training points that lie in RjR_jRj​

Recursive binary splitting

  1. top_down: starts at top of tree and successively splits predictor space,
  2. greedy: at each step the best split is chosen.

Split

This consists for a predictor j and a cutoff S, we split the region into

R1(j,s)={X∣Xj<s} and R2(j,s)={X∣Xj≥s}R_1(j,s) = \{X \mid X_j < s\} \textrm{ and } R_2(j,s) =\{X \mid X_j \ge s\} R1​(j,s)={X∣Xj​<s} and R2​(j,s)={X∣Xj​≥s}

Find the values of j and s that minimize

∑i:xi∈R1(j,s)(yi−yR1^)+∑i:xi∈R2(j,s)(yi−yR2^)\sum_{i:x_i \in{R_1(j,s)}} (y_i - \hat{y_{R_1}})+\sum_{i:x_i \in{R_2(j,s)}} (y_i - \hat{y_{R_2}}) i:xi​∈R1​(j,s)∑​(yi​−yR1​​^​)+i:xi​∈R2​(j,s)∑​(yi​−yR2​​^​)

Next repeat on one of the regions obtained

Tree pruning

  1. Grow large treeT0T_0T0​
  2. Prune back to give a subtree

Cost complexity pruning

Example workflow


then pass tune in it.

Classification

For the categorical response variable.
In a classification tree, we predict for each region, the most common level.

Criteria for aplitting


where k represent the level of k. If yi=ky_i =kyi​=k, tehn I will be 1, else will be 0

Two class measures

Example


Trees versus linear models

Advantages of trees

  1. easy to explain
  2. model human decision process
  3. nice graphics, and
  4. handle qualitative easily.

Dis-advantages of trees

  1. do not have same predictive accuracy as other methods,
  2. non-robust.

Titanic tree example




Week 9

Random forests

Motivation - high variance

General idea

Consider n independent observations:

X1,X2,…,XnX_1,X_2, \dots, X_n X1​,X2​,…,Xn​

such that

Xi∼N(μ,σ2)X_i \sim N(\mu,\sigma^2) Xi​∼N(μ,σ2)

Then

Var(X‾)=σ2nVar(\overline{X}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} Var(X)=nσ2​

Prediction models

Bagging

Classification

In the classification case, we fit the trees, and then for each tree get the predicted class for a given set of predictors, finally we use a majority vote allocation, i.e., the overall prediction it the most commonly occurring classification.

Random forests Motivation

Conside N iid random variables, XiX_iXi​, with variance σ2\sigma^2σ2, then

Var(X‾)=σ2nVar(\overline{X}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} Var(X)=nσ2​

Consider N identically distributed random variables, XiX_iXi​, with a positive pairwise correlation ρ\rhoρ, then

Var(X‾)=ρσ2+1−ρNσ2Var(\overline{X}) = \rho\sigma^2 + \frac{1-\rho}{N}\sigma^2 Var(X)=ρσ2+N1−ρ​σ2

Bagging

Consider a problem with one very strong predictor, then all the trees will probably have this predictor as one of the early splits, and so all of the trees are similar and hence the predictions are highly correlated.

Random forest

Random forests decorrelate the trees.

  1. As bagging, we use bootstrapped training samples.
  2. Each time a split is considered, then a random sample of m predictors out of p possible predictors is chosen for the candidate split. A fresh sample of m predictors is chosen each tiem a split is considered.

Example using Bostom data set


Using random forest build in tidymodels

Out-of-bag error estimation

On average each bagged tree uses about 2/3 of the observations. The remaining 1/3 of the observations not used are referred to as *out-of-bag(OOB) observations. So we can predict the value for an observation each time it is not used, and hence use this to give an overall prediction for the point

Variable importance

Random forests also have a OOB variable importance measure.

  1. For the bth tree, the OOB samples are predicted and the accuracy measured.
  2. The jth variables values for the OOB samples are permuted, and the accuracy is again calculated.
  3. The decrease in accuracy is averaged over all the trees and this is used as a measure for the jth variable.

VIP


SVM

HyperPlan


Classification using separating hyperplanes

Predictors in n×pn \times pn×p matrix X, and responses Y, Yi∈{−1,1}Y_i \in \{-1,1\}Yi​∈{−1,1}.
Suppose it is possible to find a hyperplane that separaters the training set according to class labels. Such an hyperplane is called a separating hyperplane

Properties


Maximal margin classifier

The Maximal margin classifier is the separating hyperplane that is furthest away from the training data. The distance is based on the perpendicular distance from each point to the hyperplane. The smallest distance is called the margin. Any points that lie on the margin are called support vectors

The Non-separable Case

If the points are separable, then we use a soft margin.The generalization of the maximal margin classifier to the non-separable case i s known as the support vector classifier

Non-linear decision boundaries

Rather than using the p predictors

X1,X2,…,XpX_1,X_2, \dots ,X_p X1​,X2​,…,Xp​

we could use the 2p predictors:

X1,X12,X2,,X22…,Xp,Xp2X_1,X_1^2,X_2, ,X_2^2\dots ,X_p,X_p^2 X1​,X12​,X2​,,X22​…,Xp​,Xp2​

Inner product

Generalization of inner product

Example


Fit linear


Non- linear fit


Board game svm example










Week 10

PCA

Concept

so far we have p predictor variables

X1,X2,…,XpX_1,X_2,\dots,X_p X1​,X2​,…,Xp​

and a response variable Y
From these we have used a variety of models to elucidate the relationship between the response and predictors.
In supervised learning, we have only the predictors
It is often used for exploratory data analysis

Dimendion reduction

Consider p predictors

X1,X2,…,XpX_1,X_2,\dots ,X_p X1​,X2​,…,Xp​

To examine the relationships, we could look at the (p2)\binom{p}{2}(2p​) pairwise scatterplots.
This becomes tedious quickly at p get large, e.g., p=100p = 100p=100 gives 4950 scatterplots.
We need a method that gives a low-dimensional representation the data

PCA

PCA can find a low-dimensional representation that contains as much as possible of the variation
There lower dimensional representations are called principal components

First PC

Loadings

The elements

ϕ11,ϕ21,…,ϕp1\phi_{11},\phi_{21},\dots,\phi_{p1} ϕ11​,ϕ21​,…,ϕp1​

are called the loadings

Finding first PC

Let first PC be α1TX\alpha_1^TXα1T​X and the variance / covariance of X be ∑\sum∑
First we have

var(α1TX)=α1T∑α1var(\alpha_1^TX) = \alpha_1^T\sum\alpha_1 var(α1T​X)=α1T​∑α1​

So first PC is

max α1T∑α1\textrm{max } \alpha_1^T\sum\alpha_1 max α1T​∑α1​

such that α1Tα1=1\textrm{such that } \alpha_1^T\alpha_1 = 1 such that α1T​α1​=1

Lagrange

eigenvalue

Finding the second PC

max α2T∑α2\textrm{max } \alpha_2^T\sum\alpha_2 max α2T​∑α2​

such that uncorrelated with

α1TX\alpha_1^TX α1T​X

means their Covariance is equal to zero

then we use lagrange

By similar argument maximum eigenvalue that is not first eigenvalue.

US arrests data example

Parallel coordinate plot


Apply PCA

Loadings visualization


Breast cancer example


clean up and plot parallel coordinates plot

Clustering

Clustering

To find subgroups or clusters in the data.
To achieve this we need to define what it means for two objects to be similar or different

  1. PCA: finds a low dimensional representation of the data that explains most of the variance.
  2. Clustering: finds homogeneous subgroups of the data.

K-means clustering

Goal

Split the data into a pre-specified number of groups

Set-up

  1. Pre-specify the number of groups K
  2. Each observation is assigned to exactly one cluster

Notation

Good clustering

A good clustering is one that minimises the within-cluster variation, i.e., we want to solve

minC1,C2,…,Ck{∑k=1KW(CK)}min_{C_1,C_2,\dots,C_k} \{\sum_{k=1}^{K} W(C_K)\} minC1​,C2​,…,Ck​​{k=1∑K​W(CK​)}

Within-cluster variation - Squared Euclidean distance

Clustering

Algorithm

  1. Randomly assign a number of 1 to K to each observation
  2. Iterate unitl no changing in cluster assignment:
    a. For each cluster calculate the centroid. The centroid is the vector of the sample mean of the p features for observations in the cluster
    b. Assign each observation to the cluster of the centroid that is closest.

Example - simulation

Step 2


Repeat

Penguins data example

Clustered penguins

Means information

Varinace information


Hierarchical clustering

Penguins dataset


Dendrogram

Bottom-up or agglomerative clustering

Start with leaves and combine clusters unitl trunk
Height of fusion of two observations gives an indication of how different these two observations are

Cutting


Algorithm

  1. Start with n observations and (n2)\binom{n}{2}(2n​) pairwise dissimilarities.Treat each observations as its own cluster.
  2. For $ i =n,n-1,\dots,2:
    a. Fuse the two clusters of the i clusters that are most similar
    b. Compute the new pairwise inter-cluster dissimilarites between the remaining i-1 clusters.

entry.title: data science prac
entry.content:
Week1

load package

use to load package that we need

pacman::p_load(
  tidyverse,
  tidymodels,
  vip
)

overview of the data

glimpse(data)

select column we need

mpg %>%
  select(cty, displ, drv)

or

mpg <- mpg |>
  select(cty, displ, drv)

convert between type

make drv (char) convert to factor

mpg <- 
  mpg |> 
  mutate(
    drv = factor(drv)
  )

EDA

overview

skim(mpg)

or

skimr::skim_without_charts(mpg)

different plot

single variable:

ggplot(aes(FE))

bivariate variable:

ggplot(aes(eng_displ,FE))

histogram:
used to fill color in the column and decribe the outline of column

geom_histogram(col =,fill =)

Scatter plot:

geom_point()

box plot:

geom_boxplot()

split datasets

strata will be the column that you want to split

set.seed(2023)
mpg_split <- initial_split(mpg, strata = cty)
mpg_train <- training(mpg_split)
mpg_test <- testing(mpg_split)

relationship between predictors:

select_if(is.numeric) %>%
cor()%>%
corrplot()

cor () refers to corvariance and corrplot will plot their relationships

preprocessing with recipes

car_recipe <-
recipe(FE ~ ., data = car_train) %>%
step_poly(eng_displ, degree =2, options = list(raw =TRUE))

recipe function will set the predictor and response variable, the usage will be recipe("response".,"data")
car_recipe <-car_recipe %>% prep() will be shown we trained and how much data we got
car_recipe <-car_recipe %>% juice() will show the data

set up model

linear_reg() will perform linear regression

mpg_model <- linear_reg() |> 
  set_mode("regression") |> 
  set_engine("lm")

put workflow

    workflow() |> 
    add_model(mpg_model) |> 
    add_recipe(mpg_recipe)

fit model

mpg_fit <- 
  mpg_wf |> 
  fit(mpg_train)

example model

We can get the coefficients for the linear regression using tidy().

mpg_fit |> tidy()

We can get model statistics using glance().

mpg_fit |> glance()

Assumption checking

mpg_lm <- 
  mpg_fit |> 
  extract_fit_engine()

display plot using gglm::gglm(mpg_lm) or plot(mpg_lm)

make prediction on pretrain model

create new data using tibble and using predict function to get the result

new_data <- tibble(
  displ = 3, 
  drv = "4"
)
predict(mpg_fit, new_data = new_data)

get prediction intervals, confidence interval by changing the type

predict(mpg_fit, new_data = new_data, type = "pred_int")

Variable importance plots (VIP)

vip() for plot, vi() for table

mpg_fit |> 
  extract_fit_parsnip() |> 
  vip()
mpg_fit |> 
  extract_fit_parsnip() |> 
  vi()

final fit

last_fit(mpg_fit, mpg_split) |> collect_metrics()

#Week 2
##lecture

Json convert

root folder, and file name

songs <- jsonlite::fromJSON(
    here::here('data','eurovision-lyrics.json')
)

get all row into tibble(not using for loop)

songs <-
songs %>%
map_df(parse_song, .id ="ID")

where parse_song is the function that we wriiten to convert the all detail to the tibble

arrange thing

arrange()
from big to small
arrange(-n)

count how many times it appear

count()

select string as key value and see how many time it appear

filter(str_detrct(artist,"Jed"))

check a range of integer

range(songs$years)

change / create column

songs <- songs %>% mutate(
    english_lyrics = ifelse(
        translation =="English,
        lyrics, translation
        )
    )
    ```
it means if translation == english ,we grab the lyrics, else grab the translation
### view file();
`view()`
#Week 3
### create outcome and predictor
create status as the outcome and rest is predictor
`recipe(status ~., data = credit_data)`
### Center and scale

credit_recipe %>%
step_center(all_numeric()) %>%
step_scale(all_numeric())

### skewness
![](https://hiakkaoyri1220.com/post-images/1691729614698.png)
### Missing data

vis_miss(credit_data)
gg_miss_upset(credit_data)

### set up modeimpute and meanimpute

credit_recipe %>%
step_modeimpute(all_numeric()) %>%
step_meanimpute(all_numeric())

### set up near zero

credit_recipe %>%
step_nzv(all_numeric())

### Collinearity

credit_recipe %>%
select_if(is.numeric) %>%
cor(use = "pairwise.complete.obs") %>%
corrplot()

### set up corr

credit_recipe %>%
step_corr(all_numeric())

### Dummy

credit_recipe %>%
step_dummy(all_nominal(), ~status)

#Week 4
### recipe usage
to create a recipe object,we can use `recipe()` function.

mpg_recipe <- recipe(cty ~ displ + drv, data = mpg_train)
mpg_recipe

Everything to the left of ~ is a response variable, and everything to the right of ~ is a predictor.
### Adding steps
All of the steps for pre-processing can now be added to the recipe object. They all start with `step_`. We are going to look at some of the main pre-processing steps that you might want to use.
### Quantitative variables
First we are going to center (remove the sample mean) and scale (divide by the sample standard deviation)

mpg_recipe <-
mpg_recipe |>
step_center(all_numeric_predictors()) |>
step_scale(all_numeric_predictors())
mpg_recipe


So this tells R what we want to do, and also which variables that we want to do it to. But at this stage, it has not done anything.

To get it to do something, we `prep()` the recipe.

`mpg_recipe |> prep()`

Now it has looked at the data - found the numerical predictors and also calculated any numbers it needs. To see inside the process we use `tidy()`

For all the steps use:

`mpg_recipe |> prep() |> tidy()`

For the details of an individual step give the step number

`mpg_recipe |> prep() |> tidy(n = 1)`

###Categorical variables
Now we are going to convert the categorical variables into the standard design matrix

mpg_recipe <-
mpg_recipe |>
step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors())
mpg_recipe


Again, we have a look at the overall recipe:

`mpg_recipe |> prep() |> tidy()`

And the new step:

`mpg_recipe |> prep() |> tidy(n = 3)`

Next, we are going to add an interaction term between drv and displ, to do this is a bit more complicated as after using dummy, the column drv has been replaced by two new columns drv_f and drv_r so rather than typing all the new columns in, we will use a special select function called starts_with. Also note the ~ in this case is creating you a quick function on the fly.

mpg_recipe <- mpg_recipe |>
step_interact(terms = ~starts_with("drv"):displ)
mpg_recipe


`mpg_recipe |> prep() |> tidy(n = 4)`

### Adding to workflows
So we have our recipe, we can add this to our workflow with add_recipe:

mpg_wf <- workflow()
mpg_wf <-
mpg_wf |>
add_recipe(mpg_recipe)
mpg_wf

entry.title: p1966 火柴排队(树状数组求逆序对+离散化)
entry.content:
link

通过观察是数据，我们发现 1<=n<=10^5, 0≤ 火柴高度 < 2^31, 发现需要进行离散化才能满足空间需求. 通过观察样例,我们可以发现如果想让结果最优,我们需要把大的和大的放在一起,小的和小的放在一起,同样的数字一定要放在一起,这启发了我们可以考虑数组中元素的相对大小关系. 通过构造样例,进一步发现在两个数组中,相对大小关系相同的元素组成一对一定是最优解

证明:

通过题目要求∑(ai−bi)2\sum_{}^{} (a_{i} - b_{i})^{2}∑​(ai​−bi​)2最小,等于让∑(2aibi)\sum_{}^{} (2a_{i}b_{i})∑​(2ai​bi​)最大,通过排序不等式可以证明.

那么我们可以想到对于两个数组进行相对大小关系的离散化,即把两个原数组按照从大到小的关系进行排序,并记录它们的下标.也就是说,现在对于a数组来讲,存储的是从第一大到第n大的数的下标位置. 对于b数组来讲,存储的是从第一大到第n大的数的下标位置.

那么我们希望让两个数组的序列顺序相等,那么问题就变成了将转化为b序列要交换几次可以令其等于a序列(以a数组为标准).
例子:
1 3 4 2 , 1 7 2 4
相对位置离散化后:
3 2 4 1 2 4 3 1

乱序序列 c映射之后:
1 4 2 3

先来看 c[a[i].num]=a[i].num的：

1 2 3 ... n;

那么c[a[i].num]=b[i].num的目标状态就是上面那个，

c[a[i].num]意味着c数组代表把a里面的所有相对大小的元素映射成 1-n，
c[a[i].num]=b[i].num意味着c数组代表把b里面的所有相对大小的元素映射成 1-n，
因为1到n是代表a里面的所有相对大小的元素，所以如果不一样，则代表要交换，我们的目标则是让两个序列的相对大小关系的pair一样.

下面求的时候改成了从右往左遍历，因为排序为它是前面第几大的，但因为我们要根据index来映射1 -n，但我们树状数组的模板为第几大，所以需要从右往左遍历，也就是从n - 1去找后面有多少个比他小，也就是从1 - n去找前面有多少个比他大。
每次把这个数的位置加入到树状数组中，因为是排完序之后，所以之前加入的一定比后加入的大，然后在查询当前这个数前面位置的数（是前面位置的数，要当前这个数减1，就是逆序对的个数了。

#include<bits/stdc++.h>
#define pb push_back
#define eb emplace_back
typedef long long ll;
using namespace std;
const ll p = 1e8-3;
int n;
struct node{
	int val,num;
}a[500010],b[500010];
ll tree[500010],res;
int q[500010];
	bool cmp1(node n1,node n2)
	{
		return n1.val > n2.val;
	}
	int get(int x){
		ll ans =0;
		for(;x;x-=x&-x)ans=(ans+tree[x])%p;
		return ans;
	}
	void add(int x,int k){
		for(;x<=n;x+=x&-x)tree[x]=(tree[x]+k)%p;
	}
	int main(){
	  ios::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(0);
	  cin>>n;
	  for(int i=1;i<=n;++i)
	  {
	  	cin>>a[i].val;
	  	a[i].num =i;
	  }

	  for(int i=1;i<=n;++i)
	  {
	  	cin>>b[i].val;
	  	b[i].num = i;
	  }
	  sort(a+1,a+1+n,cmp1);
	  sort(b+1,b+1+n,cmp1);
	  // for(int i=1;i<=n;++i)
	  // {
	  // 	d1[i] = a[i].num;
	  // }
	  // for(int i=1;i<=n;++i)
	  // {
	  // 	d2[i] = b[i].num;
	  //} 
	  for(int i=1;i<=n;++i)
	  {
	  	//cout<<d1[i] <<" "<<d2[i]<<endl;
	  	cout<<a[i].num <<" "<<b[i].num<<endl;
	  } 
	  for(int i=1;i<=n;i++)
	  	{
	  		q[a[i].num]=b[i].num; 
	  	}
	  for(int i=1;i<=n;i++)
	  	{
	  		cout<<q[i]<<endl; 
	  	}
	  	for(int i=n;i;--i)
	  	{
	  		add(q[i],1);
	  		res=(res+get(q[i]-1))%p;
	  		cout<<res<<endl;
	  	}
	  	cout<<res<<endl;
	return 0;
}
// #include<bits/stdc++.h>
// #define pb push_back
// #define eb emplace_back
// typedef long long ll;
// using namespace std;
// const ll p = 1e8-3;
// int n;
// struct node{
// 	int val,num;
// }a[500010],b[500010];
// ll tree[500010],res;
// int q[500010];
// 	bool cmp1(node n1,node n2)
// 	{
// 		return n1.val < n2.val;
// 	}
// 	int get(int x){
// 		ll ans =0;
// 		for(;x;x-=x&-x)ans=(ans+tree[x])%p;
// 		return ans;
// 	}
// 	void add(int x,int k){
// 		for(;x<=n;x+=x&-x)tree[x]=(tree[x]+k)%p;
// 	}
// 	int main(){
// 	  ios::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(0);
// 	  cin>>n;
// 	  for(int i=1;i<=n;++i)
// 	  {
// 	  	cin>>a[i].val;
// 	  	a[i].num =i;
// 	  }

// 	  for(int i=1;i<=n;++i)
// 	  {
// 	  	cin>>b[i].val;
// 	  	b[i].num = i;
// 	  }
// 	  sort(a+1,a+1+n,cmp1);
// 	  sort(b+1,b+1+n,cmp1);
// 	  // for(int i=1;i<=n;++i)
// 	  // {
// 	  // 	d1[i] = a[i].num;
// 	  // }
// 	  // for(int i=1;i<=n;++i)
// 	  // {
// 	  // 	d2[i] = b[i].num;
// 	  //} 
// 	  // for(int i=1;i<=n;++i)
// 	  // {
// 	  // 	//cout<<d1[i] <<" "<<d2[i]<<endl;
// 	  // 	cout<<a[i].num <<" "<<b[i].num<<endl;
// 	  // } 
// 	  for(int i=1;i<=n;i++)
// 	  	{
// 	  		q[a[i].num]=b[i].num; 
// 	  	}
// 	  // for(int i=1;i<=n;i++)
// 	  // 	{
// 	  // 		cout<<q[i]<<endl; 
// 	  // 	}
// 	  	for(int i=1;i<=n;++i)
// 	  	{
// 	  		add(q[i],1);
// 	  		res=(res+(i-get(q[i])))%p;
// 	  		// cout<<res<<endl;
// 	  	}
// 	  	cout<<res<<endl;
// 	return 0;
// }

entry.title: p2048 超级钢琴 做题记录
entry.content:
参考题解
首先可以看到，每段超级和弦都是连续的，美妙度是这段区间内所有美妙度的和。可以想到，每次求解区间和显然是不合算的，所以考虑到用前缀和。

考虑暴力，我们需要把所有满足条件的字段抽出来排个序，但这实在是不可想象。所以考虑使用贪心思想来解决这个问题。

先想预处理。我们定义 MAX(o,l,r)=max{sum(t)−sum(o−1) ∣ l≤t≤r} ,即以 o 为左端点，右端点范围是 [l, r] 的最大子段。求 sum() 就用前面说的前缀和。可以看出，o 的位置是固定的。所以 sum(o - 1)也是固定的。所以我们要求这个的最大值，只要 sum(t) 最大就可以了。即要求 sum(t)在 [l, r] 中的最大值，那怎么快速地求出这个最大值呢？很显然，这不是 RMQ 的活么。当然，具体计算的时候还要看看上界 rr 是否超过了 n 。

接下来想怎么贪心。我们可以每次都选最优的子段，这样选 kk 次显然就是我们所要的结果，那怎么找到最优解呢？用堆来将解存进去，每次堆顶的元素就是最优解。

考虑一个三元组 (o, l, r) 表示以 o 为左端点，右端点的选择区间为 [l, r] 的 情况，我用了一个 struct 来表示这个三元组，但往往实际上每个情况需要额外记录这个情况的最优解 t ，这个不麻烦，在 struct 里面敲一个构造函数就可以了。

我们假设当前最大的三元组是 (o, l, r)。最优解位置是 t。ans 累加这个三元组的贡献。由于 t 已经被选中，对于这个 o， t 已经不能重复选中，但最优解还可能存在于 t 左右的两端区间中，所以提取出 (o, l, r)之后，为了避免重复且不丧失其他较优解，我们仍然要把 (o,l,t−1) , (o, t + 1, r)扔回堆里面去。显然地，在放回去之前应该保证区间的存在，即 l = t 或 r = t的情况要进行特判。

最后实现的时候还要注意一点，RMQ 原本数组里面记录的是最优解的值，但我们查询区间最大值的时候查询的是最优解的位置。所以数组里面存的是最优解的位置，要特殊处理。

for(int i=1;i<=n;++i)
	  {
	  	if(i+l1-1<=n)
	  	{
	  		pq.push({i,i+l1-1,min(i+r1-1,n),qu(i+l1-1,min(i+r1-1,n))});
	  	}
	  }

疑问?难点?

首先我们可以将以1n为开头的长度为lr的超级和弦中

分别是最大的n个超级和弦放进队列

按值从大到小排序

此时我们取出队列中的队顶,这一定是全局最大的美妙度

解释: 结构体(i,i+l1-1,min(i+r1-1,n),qu(i+l1-1,min(i+r1-1,n)),代表这以i为左端点,从l到r这一个区间里面,最大的超级和弦(也就是上面所说的前缀和)为qu(i+l1-1,min(i+r1-1,n),
如果我们重载运算符使得堆的排序方式为 pre[n1.t] - pre[n1.o-1] < pre[n2.t] - pre[n2.o-1],那么我们就可以得到最优解

通过此题，我们可以学习到如果对st表进行最值的index的查询，前缀和的优化，贪心思路，和维护k大的值

#include<bits/stdc++.h>
#define pb push_back
#define eb emplace_back
typedef long long ll;
using namespace std;
int n,k,l1,r1;
ll f[500010][22],pre[500010];
	struct node{
		int o,l,r,t;
		friend bool operator < (const node &n1,const node &n2){
			return pre[n1.t] - pre[n1.o-1] < pre[n2.t] - pre[n2.o-1];
		}
	};
	int qu(int l,int r)
	{
		int k = log(r-l+1)/log(2);
		int x = f[l][k], y=f[r-(1<<k)+1][k];
		int index=0;
		if(pre[x] > pre[y])
		{
			index = x;
		}else
		{
			index = y;
		}
		return index;
	}
	int main(){
	  ios::sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(0);
	  cin>>n>>k>>l1>>r1;
	  for(int i=1;i<=n;++i)
	  	{
	  		cin>>pre[i];
	  		pre[i] +=pre[i-1];
	  	}
	  	//for(int i=1;i<=n;++i)cout<<pre[i];
	  for(int i=1;i<=n;++i)f[i][0] = i;
	  int t = log(n)/log(2)+1;
	  for(int j=1;j<t;++j){
	  	for(int i=1;i<=n-(1<<j)+1;++i)
	  	{
	  		int x = f[i][j-1],y =f[i+(1<<(j-1))][j-1];
	  		if(pre[x] > pre[y])
	  		{
	  			f[i][j] = x;
	  		}else
	  		{
	  			f[i][j] = y;
	  		}
	  	}
	  }
	  priority_queue<node>pq;
	  for(int i=1;i<=n;++i)
	  {
	  	if(i+l1-1<=n)
	  	{
	  		pq.push({i,i+l1-1,min(i+r1-1,n),qu(i+l1-1,min(i+r1-1,n))});
	  	}
	  }
	  ll res =0;
	  //cout<<k<<endl;
	  while(k--)
	   {
	   	auto to = pq.top();
	   	//cout<<to.t;
	   	pq.pop();
	  	res+=pre[to.t] - pre[to.o-1];
	    if(to.l !=to.t) pq.push({to.o,to.l,to.t-1,qu(to.l,to.t-1)});
	   	if(to.t !=to.r) pq.push({to.o,to.t+1,to.r,qu(to.t+1,to.r)});
	   }
	   cout<<res<<endl;
	return 0;
}
